items.front: 6-4-1: Four methods to apply longitudinal separation
items.back: 1. Depart at specified time. 2. Arrive at fix at specified time. 3. Hold at fix until specified time. 4. Change altitude at specified time or fix.

items.front: 6-4-1: Phraseology for fix crossing time restrictions
items.back: CROSS (fix) AT OR BEFORE (time) — sets a latest crossing time. CROSS (fix) AT OR AFTER (time) — sets an earliest crossing time.

5 miles DME/ATD OR 3 minutes between other aircraft. Requires one of three conditions: (1) departing follows departing from same/adjacent, (2) departing follows en route reporting fix serving departure, (3) en route follows en route reporting same fix.
10 miles DME/ATD OR 5 minutes between other aircraft. Same three conditions as subpara a.
10 miles. Requires descending aircraft leading OR climbing aircraft following (altitude change must open separation). DME mixed-use qualification applies.
5 minutes. Requires ALL three: (a) descending leads / climbing follows, (b) ≤4,000 ft apart when change started, (c) change within 10 min of following reporting fix or acknowledging crossing time.

items.front: 6-4-2c3: Climb/descend through altitude — RNAV aircraft on narrow route
items.back: 10 miles. Requires: (a) descending leads / climbing follows, (b) ≤4,000 ft apart initially, AND route width is ≤8 miles.

items.front: 6-4-2d: Fallback when a, b, or c cannot be met
items.back: 20 miles DME/ATD OR 10 minutes between other aircraft. This is the catch-all longitudinal separation minimum.

items.front: 6-4-2e: One DME/ATD aircraft, one non-DME aircraft — mixed case
items.back: 30 miles. Requires: (1) DME/ATD aircraft uses same NAVAID/waypoint the non-DME reported over, AND (2) non-DME aircraft is within 15 minutes of that NAVAID.

items.front: 6-4-2: Why must the descending aircraft lead or climbing aircraft follow in climb-through cases?
items.back: The altitude change must OPEN separation, not close it. A lead descending away or a trail climbing up into space increases safety; the opposite closes the gap.
Assign different altitudes from 10 MINUTES BEFORE until 10 MINUTES AFTER estimated passing. Discontinue only after one of the paragraph's passing conditions is met.
Both aircraft report passing NAVAIDs, DME fixes, or waypoints indicating they have passed each other.
Both aircraft report passing the same intersection/waypoint AND are at least 3 minutes apart.
Route ≤8 mi wide → 8 mi. Route >8 mi wide → 18 mi. Extended usable distance >130 mi → 30 mi.
Route ≤8 mi → 4 mi beyond (or 3 min, whichever greater). Route >8 mi → 9 mi beyond (or 3 min). Extended >130 mi → 15 mi (or 3 min).

items.front: 6-4-3: Does this procedure apply only to aircraft on the same airway?
items.back: No. It also applies to aircraft on diverging airways or radials of the same NAVAID. (Per note in paragraph.)

items.front: 6-4-4: Pilot-maintained longitudinal separation — conditions and values
items.back: Pilots must be: on SAME COURSE, in DIRECT RADIO COMMUNICATION with each other, and CONCUR. Following aircraft maintains 10 MINUTES or 20 MILES (DME/ATD with standard mixed restriction).
MAINTAIN AT LEAST ONE ZERO MINUTES/TWO ZERO MILES SEPARATION FROM (ident).

items.front: 6-4-5: What to tell an RNAV pilot when applying DME separation on VOR airways
items.back: USE DME DISTANCES. Because ATD with slant-range correction will not coincide with the direct DME readout.
